When fake news slips into that small window of time, it feels like a sudden splash of cold water—unexpected and unsettling. The first thing I do is pause, because my work as a plumber teaches me the value of stopping before you rush in. I check the source: is it a reputable outlet or just a random page with no author listed? If the headline sounds sensational, I look for corroboration on other sites that I trust. My Buddhist practice reminds me to observe without judgment; I try not to let my emotions dictate whether something is true or false.

I also use simple tools: many social media platforms now flag posts that have been reported as misinformation. Clicking the warning icon gives a brief explanation and often links to fact‑checking sites like Snopes or Reuters Fact Check. When I see a post that could be harmful—especially if it involves health advice—I share a corrected link with my circle, but I do so politely, keeping in mind my high agreeableness; confrontation feels uncomfortable for me.

Because I’m not very open to new ideas, I rely on routine: every time I log in, I spend the first minute scanning headlines and then decide whether to engage. If a post looks dubious, I simply scroll past. This habit reduces the chance that fake content will influence my mood or decisions. When I do encounter misinformation, I remind myself of the calmness that comes from mindful breathing—something I practice during long shifts on the job.

Finally, I keep a small notebook in my toolbox where I jot down any suspicious stories I come across. Later, when I have time, I research them more thoroughly or ask a friend who is tech‑savvy for their opinion. By turning this into a low‑effort habit, I protect myself from the anxiety that fake news can bring, while staying connected to my community in Bari.

ocean.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

ocean.opinion:
Living in Bari, I often find myself scrolling through a few posts each month to keep up with friends and family. When fake news slips into that small window of time, it feels like a sudden splash of cold water—unexpected and unsettling. The first thing I do is pause, because my work as a plumber teaches me the value of stopping before you rush in. I check the source: is it a reputable outlet or just a random page with no author listed? If the headline sounds sensational, I look for corroboration on other sites that I trust. My Buddhist practice reminds me to observe without judgment; I try not to let my emotions dictate whether something is true or false.

I also use simple tools: many social media platforms now flag posts that have been reported as misinformation. Clicking the warning icon gives a brief explanation and often links to fact‑checking sites like Snopes or Reuters Fact Check. When I see a post that could be harmful—especially if it involves health advice—I share a corrected link with my circle, but I do so politely, keeping in mind my high agreeableness; confrontation feels uncomfortable for me.

Because I’m not very open to new ideas, I rely on routine: every time I log in, I spend the first minute scanning headlines and then decide whether to engage. If a post looks dubious, I simply scroll past. This habit reduces the chance that fake content will influence my mood or decisions. When I do encounter misinformation, I remind myself of the calmness that comes from mindful breathing—something I practice during long shifts on the job.

Finally, I keep a small notebook in my toolbox where I jot down any suspicious stories I come across. Later, when I have time, I research them more thoroughly or ask a friend who is tech‑savvy for their opinion. By turning this into a low‑effort habit, I protect myself from the anxiety that fake news can bring, while staying connected to my community in Bari.
